topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the conference room hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. Sarah slid the binder across the polished mahogany table, her expression one of practiced, unsettling calm. "So, as we discussed, you'll be taking the lead on onboarding Mark." I stared at the binder, my jaw tightening so hard I could feel the muscle twitching in my neck. It was a blatant dismissal, a clear signal that my expertise was suddenly obsolete. I leaned back in my chair, the leather squeaking in protest, and let a slow, deliberate breath escape. "You expect me to just hand over the entire operational knowledge to someone who hasn't even finished the orientation module?" My voice was dangerously level, the volume barely above a whisper, which somehow made it sound infinitely more menacing. Every nerve ending in my body was buzzing with a hot, tight pressure. I picked up a pen, turning it over and over in my fingers until the plastic felt slick.

[story 2]

"This is your chance to really step up, Alex," Mr. Henderson boomed, clapping his hands together with an almost patronizing enthusiasm. He gestured toward the nervous-looking newcomer sitting across from me. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising up my chest, making my palms sweat. I looked at the stack of manuals—pages upon pages detailing processes I had spent the last three years perfecting. It felt like a deliberate slight, a casual erasure of my contribution. "I suppose," I managed, my tone clipped and brittle, "if I can somehow compress three years of specialized experience into a two-hour session, then yes, I suppose I can facilitate this transition." I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor. I didn't look at anyone; I just walked toward the door, my shoulders rigid, the sound of my footsteps echoing the sudden, sharp rhythm of my own rising frustration.

[story 3]

I watched the handover meeting unfold from the periphery, a spectator to my own professional dismantling. When the manager finally turned to me, eyes gleaming with expectation, I felt a profound sense of being undermined. It wasn't just the task; it was the implication—that I was no longer the authority, merely a glorified babysitter. I gripped the edge of the table until my knuckles were white, the pressure a physical manifestation of the churning feeling inside me. "Are you certain," I asked, my voice laced with an almost painful dryness, "that this is the most efficient use of resources? Because I have several high-priority deliverables scheduled for the next quarter that require my direct oversight." I didn't wait for an answer. I simply gathered my notes, the rustle of the paper sounding deafening in the sudden, heavy silence that followed my challenge.
